coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot reinforced hyperbranched polyamide proton exchange membrane for direct methanol fuel cell
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: The 4th Conference on Surface,Coatings Nanostructured Materials, 11-13 October 2019, Xian, China
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Guoxiao
Wu, Zhiguang
Xie, Ziyi
Wei, Zenglv
Li, Jing
Qu, Konggang
Li, Ying
Cai, Weiwei
coredata.dc:description: The hyperbranched macromolecule (HBM) polyamide proton exchange membrane with uniform 3D matrix topology is beneficial to the enhancement of proton conductivity. In order to extend the application of HBM in direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) are incorporated into HBM to enhance the proton/methanol selectivity of the membrane. The functional groups on GO and GQD surface would interact with –SO3H groups in HBM by the hydrogen-bond interaction and participate in the proton conductive channel construction. And the GO and GQD in the composite can effectively prevent the permeation of methanol molecules. Most important, the HBM membrane filled by GQD (GQD-HBM) can effectively avoid large scale phase separation which occurs in HBM membrane filled by GO (GO-HBM) due to the greater size of GO. Proton conductivity of GQD-HBM (0.30 S/cm) is 17% improved compared with the pristine HBM while methanol permeability is significantly reduced by ca. 50% due to the physical barrier of GQD. The proton/methanol selectivity is therefore enhanced by more than 80% and the DMFC performance is also significantly enhanced.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Direct methanol fuel cell
Proton exchange membrane
High proton/methanol selectivity
Graphene quantum dot
3D matrix topology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The image of GO and GQD solution (a), SEM images of (b) pristine HBM, (c) GO-HBM and (d) GQD-HBM through cross-section.
XRD spectra of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membranes.
Temperature dependence of proton conductivity on temperature for the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membranes.
Methanol permeability and proton/methanol selectivity of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM.
Strain-stress curves of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membranes.
The TG curves of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM.
DMFC performance of pristine HBM and GQD-HBM membranes (electrode area: 1 cm2).
Schematic diagram of the self-assembled GQD in the HBM membrane.
Water uptake, volume swelling ratio, IEC value and oxidative stability (weight retention at 80 °C) of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membranes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot reinforced hyperbranched polyamide proton exchange membrane for direct methanol fuel cell

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hyperbranched macromolecule (HBM) polyamide proton exchange membrane with uniform 3D matrix topology is beneficial to the enhancement of proton conductivity. In order to extend the application of HBM in direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) are incorporated into HBM to enhance the proton/methanol selectivity of the membrane. The functional groups on GO and GQD surface would interact with –SO3H groups in HBM by the hydrogen-bond interaction and participate in the proton conductive channel construction. And the GO and GQD in the composite can effectively prevent the permeation of methanol molecules. Most important, the HBM membrane filled by GQD (GQD-HBM) can effectively avoid large scale phase separation which occurs in HBM membrane filled by GO (GO-HBM) due to the greater size of GO. Proton conductivity of GQD-HBM (0.30 S/cm) is 17% improved compared with the pristine HBM while methanol permeability is significantly reduced by ca. 50% due to the physical barrier of GQD. The proton/methanol selectivity is therefore enhanced by more than 80% and the DMFC performance is also significantly enhanced.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) is an effective energy conversion technology and has exhibited promising application potential in the various fields [1–3]. Compared to the lithium battery, DMFC can provide uninterrupted power with much higher energy density [4–6]. As one of the core components of DMFC, proton exchange membrane (PEM) directly affects the overall performance of the DMFC system [7–9]. By considering the proton conductivity, mechanical strength and stability, perfluorosulfonic acid PEMs, represented by Nafion, has been used in most currently commercial DMFC [10]. However, the fully release of the energy density of methanol (4.5 kWh/L) is hindered by the shortages of the Nafion membrane, including the low proton/methanol selectivity and high cost [11–14]. To overcome these disadvantages, cost-effective PEM materials with low methanol permeation, high proton conductivity and great mechanical/oxidative stabilities are widely developed [15–17], including sulfonated polyarylene ether nitriles (SPEN) [18,19], polybenzimidazole (PBI) [20,21], sulfonated poly (ether ether ketone) (SPEEK) [3,22–26] and sulfonated polyamide based membrane [27–29]. In our previous study, a hyperbranched polyamide macromolecular (HBM) proton exchange membrane with hierarchical proton conductive channels (PCCs) was synthesized and structural optimized [30]. By simply adjusting the monomers, the proton conductivity of HBM with 3D matrix topology was enhanced to 0.26 S/cm at 80 °C and the methanol permeability was also reduced.

To further enhance the proton/methanol selectivity of the HBM membrane for the application in DMFC, inorganic hybrid modification is considered in this work since it has been recognized as the most effective way to improve methanol permeation resistant [31–34]. Among the numerous inorganic modifiers, graphene oxide (GO) is currently the most widely researched one due to the outstanding mechanical property and atomic layered structure [4,35–37]. At the same time, the hydrophilic oxygenated functional groups make GO suitable for the formation of hydrogen-bonding interactions to assist the proton conduction channel (PCC). Unfortunately, the GO sheets incorporated into the composite membrane would break the continuity of original Nafion PCCs ascribed to the huge size of GO [38,39]. Graphene quantum dot (GQD), which can be fabricated from GO sheets with nanometer size [40,41], is therefore considered as the alternative modifier for HBM modification in this work. Since the size of GQD is much smaller than that of the HBM macromolecule, the GQD with oxygenated functional groups can be assembled with the original Nafion PCC in the composite membrane during the membrane fabrication process. The GQD filled into the HBM can form abundant hydrogen-bonding network with the HBM matrix. The hydrogen-bonding network facilitated the transport of proton inside the HBM macromolecule and between the HBM macromolecules. As a result, the efficiency of proton transfer can be improved. As a result, the efficiency of proton transfer can be improved. Methanol permeation through the HBM membrane can also be suppressed greatly due to the physical blocking effect of GQD (Scheme 1 ). As a result, the proton/methanol selectivity of the GQD-HBM was significantly enhanced and the DMFC performance was also remarkably improved. Above all, the GQD-HBM membrane exhibited promising application potential as proton exchange membrane for DMFC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of HBM, GO and GQD
ce:section.ce:para: HBM was synthesized following the procedure used in a previous work [42]. Briefly, all the solid reagents were dried in the vacuum oven. The molar ration of trimesic acid (TA) and p-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (p-DSA) was 1.05:1. The TA (450.3 mg), p-DSA (384.1 mg) and LiCl (320 mg) were added into a round-bottom flask with two necks. The flask was vacuumed by the diaphragm pump, and argon was flushed into the flask. The triphenylphosphite (1 mL), N-methyl-2-prrolidone (4 mL), pyridine (3 mL) were also added into the flask. The reaction was conducted at 100 °C for 2 h under the protection of argon. Then the solid product was washed by deionized water and methanol three times, and the product was extracted by filtration. Finally, the product was placed in the vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h, and grind the product into the power in a mortar.

According to the modified Hummers' method, the Graphene oxide sheets (GO) was synthesized with the nature graphite as the precursor. In the furnace, the GO sheets were reduced to obtain graphene sheets (GSs) at 300 °C for 2 h under nitrogen conditions. The GSs were further oxidized by socked in concentrated sulfuric acid (10 mL) and nitric acid (10 mL) for 20 h under low-intensity ultrasonic conditions. Then the mixture was poured into 300 mL deionized water and filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to obtain the oxidation products. The oxidized GSs were mixed in 40 mL deionized water and the pH of mixture was adjust to 8 by the ammonium hydroxide. The mixture was poured into 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) high pressure reactor at 200 °C for 10 h. Finally, the mixture was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and the brown GQD suspension was separated. The image of the GO and GQD solution was displayed in Fig. 1 a.

According to the solution casting method, the GO (0.1 wt%) and GQD (0.1 wt%) were added into HBM/DMSO solution separately to prepare the GO-HBM and GQD-HBM composite membrane. The mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 48 h to obtain homogeneous solution. Then the solution was poured into the glass Petri dish at 80 °C in oven. The solvent evaporated and the composite membrane was peeled off the Petri dish.

According the four probe AC impedance method, the proton conductivity of all the membrane was measured using the CHI760E workstation. The membrane was cut into long strips and was assembled in the testing mold. The test was carried out at 20–80 °C and 100% RH.
(1)
σ =
d
R t w
where, σ is the proton conductivity (S/cm), R (Ω) is the resistance, d (cm), t (cm) and w (cm) represent the width of the membrane, the distance between the electrodes and thickness of membrane respectively.

The methanol permeability testing was performed by the H-shaped mold with two liquid chambers. One chamber was filled with 1 M methanol and the other with equal volume of deionized water. The two chambers were separated by the proton exchange membrane with magnetic agitation at room temperature. A certain volume of liquid was taken from each side chamber every 1 h and the methanol content in the water was detected by gas chromatography. The methanol permeability was calculated by following equation:
(2)
p =
1 A
×
V B
C A
×
Δ C
Δ t
where, p (cm2/s) is the methanol permeability coefficient, l (cm), A (cm2), V B (cm3) and C A (mol/cm3) are the membrane thickness, the diffusion area, the volume of the compartment and the initial methanol concentration in the chambers respectively. ΔC (mol/cm3) stands for the methanol concentration difference in the chambers between two chambers, and Δt (s) is the time interval.

The microstructure characterization of the membrane was carried out by the field emission scanning electron microscopy (HITACHI SU8010); the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra was obtained on the Bruker AXS D8-Focus X-ray diffractometer; the thermal stability of membrane was tested on NetzschSTA 409 PC instrument under nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min; the Labthink Testing System was used to value the mechanical stability.

The water uptake and volume swelling represent the weight and volume changes of membrane before and after water absorption. The pretreated membrane was cut into regular rectangles. The membrane was soaked in deionized water at room temperature and the weight (Wwet) and volume (Vwet) of the membrane was accurately measured. Then the membrane was dried at 80 °C and the weight (Wdry) and volume (Vdry) of the membrane was recorded. The WU and VS were calculated by the follow equations:
(3) WU = (Wwet − Wdry)/Wdry
(4) VS = (Vwet − Vdry)/Vdry

The Fenton reagent (3% H2O2 containing 3 ppm FeSO4), as a strong oxidizer, was used to evaluate the oxidative stability of membrane. The membrane was soaked in Fenton's reagent at 80 °C for 24 h, and the remained weight was measured.

According the catalyst-coated membrane method, Pt/C (40 wt% Pt) and PtRu/C (40 wt% Pt + 20 wt% Ru) were sprayed on cathode (Pt loading: 0.8 mg/cm2) and anode (PtRu loading: 1.2 mg/cm2) side, respectively. The catalyst coated membrane was assembled in the testing device with two carbon papers covered at both side of membrane. The DMFC testing were conducted on the MiniTest 3000 Fuel Cell System (TOYO Corporation) at room temperature. The flow rate of O2 gas and 1 M methanol was 100 mL/min and 1 mL/min.

In order to check the microscopic phase change of the prepared membrane, cross-section SEM images of the three membranes (pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM) was taken as depicted in Fig. 1. It can be observed that the pristine HBM possesses the smooth cross-section. With the addition of GO, the phase separation (black area in SEM image) occurs inside the GO-HBM composite membrane due to the huge size of GO sheets. Different from GO-HBM, the GQD-HBM membrane is much more uniform ascribed to the well dispersion of GQD in HBM membrane. It is critical to maintain the continuity of proton conductive channels in the GQD-HBM.

The effect of GO and GQD on the microstructure of HBM molecular was also evaluated. Shown in Fig. 2 were the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the three membranes. The broad peak appeared at around 2θ = 26° is assigned to the partial crystallinity of HBM macromolecules in the membranes. It is worth noting that there is no significant change except the characteristic peaks shifted to lower angle while the GO and GQD were doped in HBM. According to the Bragg's equation (2d sinθ = nλ), it is deduced that the d space is swollen by the GO and GQD which participated in construction of proton conductive channel.

As the basal performances of the PEM, the water uptake (WU) and volume swelling (VS), IEC value and oxidative stability were measured, the result were listed in Table 1 . Compared with the pristine HBM membrane, the GQD-HBM membrane exhibited almost the same WU but the significantly decreased VS ratio. The dimensional stability of GQD-HBM membrane was significantly improved due to the strong hydrogen-bonding interactions between GQD and the HBM molecules. On the contrary, the VS ratio of the GO-HBM was increased due to the severe phase separation in the GO-HBM. Compared with the pristine HBM, the IEC values of the two composite membranes was only slightly decreased after the filling of GO and GQD.

Compared in Fig. 3 are the dependence of proton conductivity on temperature for the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membranes at 100% relative humidity (RH). It can be observed that the GQD-HBM exhibits remarkably better proton conductivity than that of the pristine HBM membrane. This is resulted from the hydrogen-bonding interactions between GQD surface and HBM molecules participate in the reconstruction of the PCCs and improve the efficiency of proton conductivity. But the proton conductivity of the GO-HBM membrane is significantly reduced compared with the pristine HBM membrane. The main reason is that GO, whose proton conductivity is not as well as that of HBM, with large size would break the continuity of the original proton conductive channels. Although GO can form the abundant hydrogen-bonding network with HBM matrix, the destruction of original proton conductive channels plays a primary role when affecting on the proton conductivity. The proton conductivity of the GQD-HBM membrane measured at 30 °C is high to 0.12 S/cm, which is 9% greater than of the pristine HBM membrane. With the temperature increased to 80 °C, which is the typical operation temperature for PEMFC, proton conductivity of the GQD-HBM membrane is enhanced to 0.30 S/cm, 15% greater than that of the pristine HBM membrane.

Methanol permeability is one of the most key parameters of PEM by considering the application in DMFC since the permeated methanol would lead to strong decline of the cell voltages and efficiency. In general, the enhancement in proton conductivity tends to result in an increase in methanol permeation because the methanol molecules can permeate by protons conductive channels during the DMFC operation. Provided in Fig. 4 are methanol permeability values of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membranes. It can be found that the GQD and GO filled composite membrane can effectively block the permeation of methanol molecules as desired. This result indicate that GO and GQD have well physical barrier effect on methanol molecules. And the GQD-HBM membrane performed better than the GO-HBM membrane because there is no phase separation in the GQD-HBM membrane. Methanol permeability is significantly reduced from 4.86 × 10−7 cm2/s of the pristine HBM membrane to 2.9 × 10−7 cm2/s of the GQD-HBM membrane.

The ratio of proton conductivity to methanol permeation coefficient is defined as the selectivity of the membranes, which is considered to be an important factor to estimate the PEM overall performance. Proton/methanol selectivity of the pristine HBM, GO-HBM and GQD-HBM membrane are compared in Fig. 4. The proton/methanol selectivity of GQD-HBM is high to 4.2 × 105 S/cm3, which almost doubled compared with the pristine HBM. And the selectivity of GO-HBM is slightly lower than that of pristine HBM. Although GO and GQD have similar microstructure, the effect on the overall performance of composite membrane is quite different. Compared with GO, the GQD is the batter modified materials for Nafion membrane.

The optimized composite membrane was studied in DMFC device (Fig. 7 ). The DMFC equipped with the GQD-HBM membrane exhibits significantly higher open voltage than pristine HBM due to the remarkably enhanced methanol permeation resistance. With current applied, cell voltage of the GQD-HBM is higher than that of the HBM. The GQD-HBM exhibited better fuel cell performance with the maximum output power density of 20.5 mW/cm2, which is 41% higher than that of pristine HBM membrane.

In summary, the overall performance of hyperbranched HBM membrane is significantly enhanced by filling the GQD into the HBM matrix. Compared with GO, the GQD with smaller size can effectively avoid large scale phase separation of composite membrane. The proton can be conducted efficiently by the synergies between HBM and GQD while the methanol molecules are blocked. As a result, the proton/methanol selectivity of the GQD-HBM membrane is almost doubled compared to that of the pure HBM membrane. Most important, the GQD-HBM still maintains good thermal, mechanical and oxidative stability. The DMFC equipped with GQD-HBM membrane exhibits excellent fuel cell performance with the maximum power density of 20.5 mW/cm2. Above all, the GQD-HBM membrane exhibits promising application potential as proton exchange membrane in DMFC.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21703211 and 21875224) and Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LGG19B030001).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct liquid fuel cells: a review

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of nano-size of functionalized silica on overall performance of swelling-filling modified Nafion membrane for direct methanol fuel cell application

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Overview of hybrid membranes for direct-methanol fuel-cell applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Targeted filling of silica in Nafion by a modified in situ sol-gel method for enhanced fuel cell performance at elevated temperatures and low humidity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu G, Li S, Li J, Liu Z, Li Y, Xiong J, et al. Targeted filling of silica in Nafion by a modified in situ sol-gel method for enhanced fuel cell performance at elevated temperatures and low humidity. Chem Commun. 2019;55:5499-5502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ion exchange membranes: new developments and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ran J, Wu L, He Y, Yang Z, Wang Y, Jiang C, et al. Ion exchange membranes: New developments and applications. J Membr Sci. 2017;522:267-291.
Steele BC, Heinzel A. Materials for fuel-cell technologies. Nature. 2001;414:345-352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high performance polyamide-based proton exchange membrane fabricated via construction of hierarchical proton conductive channels
